title: Checking vs. Savings Accounts
summary: Two accounts, two very different jobs.
doThisToday: If you have a bank account, check whether it's checking, savings, or both — and notice which one your debit card is connected to.

cards.prompt: Most banks offer two basic account types: checking and savings. They're not interchangeable.
cards.explanation: Checking is for money you're actively spending — your debit card pulls from it. Savings is for money you're setting aside and not touching often.

cards.prompt: Sort each kind of money into Checking or Savings.

items.label: Money for paying your phone bill
Money you're not touching for years

cards.explanation: Checking = money moving frequently. Savings = money sitting and growing until you need it. Mixing the two makes it way too easy to accidentally spend your goals.

cards.prompt: Your bank deposits are actually protected by the government.
cards.frontText: What federal program insures your bank deposits if the bank itself fails?
cards.backText: FDIC insurance (Federal Deposit Insurance Corporation), created in 1933 after Great Depression bank runs wiped out people's savings. It covers up to $250,000 per depositor, per bank — so if your bank fails, your money (up to that limit) is still safe.
cards.explanation: Look for "FDIC insured" (or "NCUA insured" for credit unions) when picking a bank — it's a free, built-in safety net.

cards.prompt: True or false: savings accounts typically earn some interest over time, while basic checking accounts usually earn little to none.
cards.explanation: Banks pay you a small amount for keeping money in savings, since they use those deposits elsewhere. Checking accounts are built for movement, not growth, so they rarely pay much interest.

cards.prompt: You keep $100 in a savings account earning 2% interest for one year. Roughly how many dollars of interest do you earn (ignore any compounding for now)?
cards.explanation: $100 × 2% = $2. Not exciting on its own — but you'll see in the next unit how this same idea gets a lot more powerful over many years.

cards.prompt: Simple rule of thumb: checking for spending, savings for everything you're not touching this week.
cards.explanation: Some people even use multiple savings accounts — one for emergencies, one for a car, one for a trip — to keep goals visually separate. You'll build on this idea more soon.
